schools.summary: Matthew Henry reads the reign as Israel's buried glory — the calf-idolatry that levelled a chosen people with the heathen — yet with sparks of honour, a praying people God is still near to.
Clarke untangles the chronology: seventeen years = fourteen alone plus three in co-regency with Joash, anticipating the discrepancy at v.10.
Gill dates the accession to the year Joash of Judah was repairing the temple, overlapping the two Joashes.
Keil and Delitzsch reconcile the regnal figures and press the moral law: the more obstinately the sin of Jeroboam is continued, the heavier the punishment God brings by Hazael and Benhadad.
Gill glosses the 'evil' as idolatry — the persistent calf-worship the king 'departed not' from.
Keil and Delitzsch trace the judgment by Hazael and Benhadad and its turn to mercy, identifying the coming 'saviour' with the successors Jehoash and Jeroboam, not an angel or Elisha.
Gill insists the wrath falls on the whole nation, for the people were 'doing as their kings did' — the idolatry was popular, not merely royal.
Ellicott glosses 'besought' as 'stroked the face of' (cf. Exod. 32:11), hears the Exodus language of 'seeing the oppression,' and notes the answer was delayed to the reigns of Joash and Jeroboam II.
Matthew Henry makes it a lesson on prayer: kings must be beggars at God's door, and God is swift to show mercy, looking back to the ancient covenant.
Gill: the king applied not to the calves but to the Lord, who heard not for his sake or his merely external repentance, but because his oppressed people were still his own.
Ellicott names Jeroboam II the grandson as the saviour (cf. 2 Kings 14:27); Keil and Delitzsch take it of both Jehoash and Jeroboam; 'dwelt in tents' means a return to the open country in peace.
Gill identifies the saviour as Joash the son, ruling out an angel, a captain, Elisha, or Jehoahaz himself.
Clarke agrees the saviour was 'undoubtedly Joash,' whose wars close the chapter.

debates.question: Who is the 'saviour' the LORD gave Israel?

sides.position: Joash the son of Jehoahaz, whose three victories fulfil Elisha's word
Jeroboam II the grandson, by whose hand Jehovah saved Israel
both successors together, Jehoash and Jeroboam, as one dynastic gift

schools.summary: Ellicott renders the clause 'the Asherah stood in Samaria' — the sacred tree of Assyrian art — and observes that returning prosperity fed the revived idolatry; Keil traces the Asherah to Ahab's day.
Clarke identifies the grove as Asherah/Astarte still worshipped; Wesley traces it to the grove Ahab planted for Baal, which Deut. 7:5 required destroyed.
Gill calls the persistence in calf-worship 'great ingratitude,' noting the Syriac/Arabic variant 'he departed not' (Jehoahaz).
Keil and Ellicott take Jehovah as the subject of 'he had not left,' reading the tiny remnant as a total disarmament that made resistance hopeless — Israel 'trodden like dust.'
Matthew Henry: 'Have the thousands of Israel come to this?' — the despicable muster is the debasing of a debauched nation.
Gill makes the king of Syria the one who left Israel so few, reading the verse as a parenthesis.

debates.question: Who is the subject of 'he had not left' Israel so few?

schools.summary: Wesley: the king's valour is noted precisely to show Israel was defeated not from his cowardice but from the righteous judgment of God upon their apostasy.
Ellicott and Keil gloss 'his might' as prowess in the Syrian wars, conceding he showed it 'although he had been overcome.'
Gill: a man of courage, yet unsuccessful 'because the Lord was not with him, but against him.'
Ellicott: 'slept with his fathers' means simply 'lay down as his fathers did' — a formula of burial and continuity, used even of Amaziah who died violently, not a verdict of peaceful death.
Gill: he 'died, as they did' — the common lot in the settled biblical idiom.

themes: a king's death versus a prophet's

schools.summary: Ellicott judges 'thirty-seventh' a textual slip, preferring the Aldine LXX's 'thirty-ninth' to fit v.1's figures.
Gill reconciles the dates by a two- or three-year co-regency, so Jehoahaz lived to see his prayer answered in his son.
Clarke, following Calmet, has Joash associated with his father two years before his death, the sixteen years including the co-regency.
Matthew Henry: none of the worst kings, yet the calf-idolatry made him 'evil in the sight of the Lord'; his one honour to Elisha outweighs his wars in God's book.

debates.question: How is 'the thirty-seventh year' to be reconciled with v.1?

sides.position: a co-regency of Joash with his father accounts for the years
a textual slip; the Aldine LXX's 'thirty-ninth' is correct

schools.summary: Keil links the verse to the fuller record of Joash's war with Amaziah (2 Kings 14:8ff.); Ellicott notes 'in it he walked' has a collective pronoun — the nation, not just the king, walks in the sin.
Gill: Joash is described 'in the same words' as his father — the unchanged formula for the unchanged sin.
Matthew Henry: the one evil of calf-idolatry marks his name with infamy, for God's judgment is according to truth, not men's estimate.
Ellicott notes the notice is repeated at 2 Kings 14:15-16, reads 'how he fought,' and points to the Amaziah war of 2 Kings 14:8ff.
Clarke fills in from 2 Chronicles 25 the total defeat of Amaziah, the sack of Jerusalem, and the plunder of the Temple treasures.
Gill: as yet only Joash's sinful acts are recorded; the annals lie ahead.

Ellicott notes the anomalous 'Jeroboam sat upon his throne' (vs. the usual formula), the Talmud/Kimchi conjecture of a co-regency for fear of revolt, and the 'tombs of the kings' at Samaria; Keil explains the notice is placed early to clear the stage for Elisha's death.
Gill glosses the opening clause simply: Joash 'died.'

themes: the reign closed out of place

schools.summary: Matthew Henry meditates on the long, then obscure, ministry of Elisha — useful to the last though not famous — and on the king's tears as real evidence of good, mingled with self-interest since the prophet was the kingdom's true defence.
Ellicott renders 'he was to die,' pictures the king 'coming down' and weeping over the prophet's face, and judges Elisha to have been more to Israel than chariots and horsemen.
Wesley marks the sixty-year ministry, a burning and shining light; Clarke dates the sickness to about the tenth year of Joash.
Gill: the prophets do not live for ever; Elisha died and was not translated like Elijah.

Keil lays out the whole sign-act — stretch the bow, shoot eastward, 'an arrow of salvation against the Syrians,' with victory promised at Aphek; Ellicott notes the weapons came from a royal attendant.
Clarke: the bow, arrows, and smiting are all emblematical, indicative of Israel's deliverance from Syria.
Gill: these were the usual weapons of war, had from the king's guards if not the prophet's house.
Ellicott explains the archer's posture and reads Elisha's hands on the king's hands as investing the act with prophetic character and consecrating the king to his task.
Matthew Henry: the laid-on hands teach the king to depend on God for strength — the trembling hands of the dying prophet gave the arrow more force than the king's own strength.
Gill notes it was the king's left hand that steadied the bow.
Ellicott renders 'an arrow of victory for Jehovah, and over Aram,' sets the promised triumph at Aphek (the scene of former defeats), and limits 'consumed' to the army there, not all Syria.
Clarke shows the shot draws on the ancient custom of hurling a spear to declare war (Alexander, Virgil's Turnus, the Roman pater patratus); Wesley reads it as a token of God's purpose against Syria.
Matthew Henry: the arrow is both a commission to attack and a promise of success — 'God commands deliverance, and who can hinder?'
Gill fixes the eastward window toward Syria and the trans-Jordan lands to be recovered.

themes: the arrow of the LORD's deliverance

schools.summary: Ellicott renders 'smite earthwards' as striking a fallen foe, notes 'three' a sacred number, and prefers the LXX's fuller opening marking a distinct second sign.
Wesley: this second sign declares the number of victories; Clarke suggests the king's smiting only thrice may itself have been 'of the Lord,' who had determined but three victories.
Matthew Henry: the king smote thrice from foolish tenderness or contempt, and 'by contemning the sign he lost the thing signified.'

Ellicott sets the scene against ancient divination by arrows (cf. 1 Samuel 20:35), reading Elisha's anger as the disappointed zeal of the man and patriot, while the outcome betokens Jehovah's purpose (Prov. 16:33).
Gill: the number was left to the king's own will to reveal his spirit; his stopping at three showed the coldness of his zeal.
Clarke cautions that the prophet's wrath does not prove God was wroth or had decreed only three victories.
Matthew Henry: not straitened in God's power, why be straitened in his own endeavours? — a grief to see men forsake their own mercies.

debates.question: Was the limit of three victories the king's forfeit or God's decree?

sides.position: the king's free, lukewarm act forfeited fuller victory and revealed his heart
God had determined only three victories and overruled the king's hand to that end
both — the king's spontaneous impulse, and the LORD disposing of it

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch note the great span of Elisha's ministry (over forty years from his call under Ahab) and the annual Moabite raids that close the verse, a token of Israel's continued exposure.
Gill gathers the burial traditions (Samaria/Sebaste, or Carmel) and the disputed dating, inclining with Kimchi to an early death in the co-regency.

themes: the death and burial of Elisha
died like other men, not translated
death as the backdrop to resurrection

schools.summary: Ellicott narrates the hasty burial and the raiders (cf. Mark 16:3-4), rendering 'and he lived,' and quotes Bähr that Elisha 'even in the grave is avouched the servant of God'; Keil reads the miracle as the seal of Elisha's promise.

Wesley: the miracle honours the prophet, seals his doctrine, and pledges eternal life; Clarke insists the power was God's, not the bones', and denounces the relic-system built on this single unrepeatable act.
Gill notes they were on the way to bury the man when the raiders came.

outliers.note: Turns the miracle into an anti-relic polemic: the first and last true miracle by a dead man's bones, wrongly made the foundation of the 'popish' system of working relics.

themes: the power of God not the relic
the prophet vindicated in the grave

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil read the verse as a pluperfect resumption — 'Hazael had oppressed' — carrying the reader back to vv.3 and 7 and framing the mercy that follows.
Gill takes 'all the days of Jehoahaz' of the years Hazael oppressed before Joash's co-regency.

Clarke finds an ascending tenderness in the three Hebrew verbs (affection, yearning bowels cf. Jer. 31:20, turned face) and names the covenant reason — the Messiah and the light for the Gentiles; Wesley: God's slowness is to the advantage of his mercy, not against his justice.

Ellicott judges the verse the compiler's own remark, the 'as yet'/'until now' betraying that he wrote after the northern kingdom had perished; Keil grounds the sparing in the covenant with the patriarchs.
Gill: grace shown notwithstanding their apostasy and the idolatry of the calves.

Ellicott explains Ben-hadad as a divine throne-title (not a bloodline), synchronises the reign with Samas-Rimmon of Assyria (who made no western campaigns), and judges this Benhadad a feebler king than Hazael.
Gill identifies him as Benhadad III, the honourable old name retained in Hazael's new dynasty.

Ellicott argues the recovered cities were west of Jordan (the trans-Jordan already lost to Hazael in Jehu's day, 2 Kings 10:32), the fuller restoration reserved for Jeroboam II (2 Kings 14:25), and ties 'beat him' to the 'smite' of v.19.
Clarke: the three victories exactly fulfil Elisha's prediction (v.19); God accomplished both his word of judgment and of mercy, and Israel learned she could do nothing without God.
Gill locates the recovered cities in Gilead and Bashan — the trans-Jordan land of Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh.

sides.position: the trans-Jordan territory of Gilead and Bashan (Reuben, Gad, half-Manasseh)
cities west of Jordan only, the eastern lands recovered later by Jeroboam II

themes: the word of judgment and of mercy
